verdict_reasoning: BAX is canonically a cytosolic/cytoplasmic pro-apoptotic BCL-2 family protein that exists predominantly as an inactive monomer in healthy cells, sometimes loosely associated with the cytoplasmic face of the OMM. Upon apoptotic activation, BAX undergoes conformational change, oligomerizes, and inserts into the mitochondrial outer membrane and plasma membrane outer leaflet, permeabilizing both. During apoptosis and ICD, BAX-exposed epitopes on the outer leaflet of the PM and at the OMM are accessible extracellularly. Anti-BAX antibodies (e.g., clone 6A7 recognizing activated BAX) are routinely used to detect surface-exposed BAX on apoptotic cells by flow cytometry on intact non-permeabilized cells — confirming outer-leaflet accessibility under cell-death conditions. Additionally, cancer-cell ecto-display of BAX has been documented. This is squarely cell_state_induced (apoptosis/ICD-triggered PM translocation). At baseline in healthy, non-apoptotic cells, BAX is cytoplasmic/inner-leaflet-associated, not stably surface-exposed. No GPI anchor, TM domain, or canonical surface topology; no lysosomal exocytosis story; no germline restriction; no wash-resistant TM-partner tethering.
